stimulus:
Average percent of maximal acceleration used by various small mammal species when foraging or fleeing predators, grouped by percentage range.

Percent of maximal accelerationForaging (species)Fleeing (species)
30–3920
40–4910
50–5911
60–6902
70–7912
80–8903
90–10016

One might assume that small mammals always flee predators at their maximum possible acceleration, but the metabolic cost of full-output sprints can disfavor that strategy even in escape contexts, as evidenced by the fact that ______
stem: Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to complete the text?

answerOptions.content: multiple small mammal species use, on average, less than 90% of their maximal acceleration even while fleeing predators.
most small mammal species use about the same percentage of their maximal acceleration when foraging as when fleeing predators.
more small mammal species use 90–100% of their maximal acceleration while foraging than use any lower percentage.
at least four small mammal species use less than 100% of their maximal acceleration while foraging.

rationale: Choice A is the best answer because the table shows several species fleeing at well below 90% of maximal acceleration (1 species at 50–59%, 2 at 60–69%, 2 at 70–79%, 3 at 80–89% — eight species total below 90%). This directly supports the claim that metabolic cost can dissuade full-speed escape. Choice B is contradicted by the data (foraging skews lower, fleeing skews higher). Choice C is wrong (only 1 species forages at 90–100%). Choice D is true but does not concern the escape context.
